summary: The author achieved 1st place by combining a 3D segmentation pipeline with an object detection approach, primarily using MONAI's FlexibleUnet with ResNet34 and EfficientNet-B3 encoders. A core architectural insight was computing the loss on the penultimate feature map of a partly U-Net rather than the final output, which proved more accurate and eliminated the need for Gaussian heatmap targets. The solution also introduced a rank-based scaling technique to align feature map distributions from diverse models, enabling seamless blending before object detection post-processing. Training relied on weighted CrossEntropy, custom MixUp augmentations, and threshold calibration across 7 experiment-based folds.
extensive_summary:
## Approach Overview
The solution leverages a hybrid pipeline combining 3D segmentation and object detection models built entirely within the MONAI framework. Rather than relying on external or simulated data, the author focused on architectural tweaks, loss function design, and a novel ensembling strategy to maximize performance on 3D cryo-ET imaging data.

## Model Architecture & Training
The core segmentation model is a **partly U-Net** implemented via MONAI's `FlexibleUnet` with ResNet34 and EfficientNet-B3 encoders. Instead of standard U-Net heads, the author computed the loss on the penultimate feature map, finding it more accurate than the final output. This eliminates the need for Gaussian heatmap targets; instead, single pixels are used as targets with a heavily weighted positive class (weight 256 vs. background weight 1). Training uses weighted CrossEntropy, cosine LR schedule (peak 0.001), mixed precision, and an effective batch size of 32. Input patches are 96x96x96, with loss computed on 48x48x48 outputs. Deep supervision on lower levels was tested but offered negligible gains.

## Data Processing & Augmentations
Raw 3D images (630x630x184) undergo standard normalization (subtract mean, divide by std) before being split into patches. To prevent overfitting during training from scratch, the pipeline employs MONAI's `RandomCrop`, axis-wise `Flip`, `rotation`, and a custom `MixUp` implementation. Validation patches are stored in RAM for efficiency.

## Ensembling Strategy
Combining segmentation and object detection outputs was challenging due to distribution mismatches in their feature maps. The author developed a rank-based scaling technique: for each class, pixel values from both models are sorted, and values from model B are replaced with the corresponding rank-matched values from model A. This aligns their distributions, allowing direct feature map blending before the object detection post-processing stage. Models were exported via JIT or TensorRT for a 200% speedup, enabling a larger ensemble.
competition_flow: Raw 3D cryo-ET volumes are normalized and split into patches, fed into MONAI-based partly U-Net and object detection models trained with weighted CrossEntropy and custom augmentations, then their feature maps are rank-scaled and blended before final object detection post-processing and submission.
data_processing: Standard normalization (subtract mean, divide by std) on 630x630x184 3D images before splitting into 96x96x96 patches.
Training augmentations: RandomCrop, axis-wise Flip, rotation, and a custom MixUp implementation.
No external or simulated data used.
loss_functions: pixel-wise loss on penultimate feature map
cv_strategy: 7 folds split by experiment, with individual class thresholds optimized via grid-search per epoch and recalibrated using OOF predictions across folds.
ensembling: Predictions from segmentation and object detection models were aligned using a rank-based scaling technique to match their feature map distributions, then directly blended before object detection post-processing, with models exported via JIT or TensorRT to enable a larger ensemble.
insights: The penultimate feature map yields higher accuracy than the final output layer.
Box regression provides negligible gains because particles of the same type share similar sizes.
Loss function design is more critical than model size for this task.
Including an unscored class (beta-amylase) improves differentiation from a closely related scored class (beta-galactosidase).
critical_findings: Using the penultimate feature map for loss computation surprisingly outperforms the final output layer.
Relying on box regression yields negligible improvements due to consistent particle sizes within classes.
Training on an unscored class (beta-amylase) significantly aids the model in distinguishing it from a scored class (beta-galactosidase).
